coredata.dc:description: Bimodal magnetic-fluorescent materials for diagnostic imaging needs surface-engineered nanoparticles with great biosafety, pronounced colloidal stability, high magnetic moments, and strong photoluminescence (PL) emission. This work presents polymer-coated nanoparticles (PCNPs) based on manganese ferrites covered with a thin shell of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for magnetic-resonance and fluorescent dual mode imaging of cancerous tumors in vivo. An in situ thermolysis of metal oxalates and phenylenediamine in diphenyl ether allows for the facile synthesis of hybrid magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles. They possess an average size of 55 ± 5 nm with strong and excitation-independent PL emission at about 600 nm with a saturation magnetization of ∼ 19 emu/g. In vivo dispersion and biosafety of the hybrid nanoparticles are improved by a polymer-coating strategy with the aid of Tween polysorbate. In vitro studies for SK-OV-3 cells reveal that PCNPs are not toxic up to a high concentration of 125 µg/mL. H&E-stained sections of vital organs of animals treated with dosages of 5 mg/kg fail to present any histopathological changes after 24 h post-injection. The fluorescence intensity in the tumor tissue region appears to increases over time and the tumor becomes clearly visible after 60 min. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of phantoms and animal cases also indicates the high efficiency of PCNPs as an T 2 contrast agent in clinical diagnosis. Therefore, the developed PCNPs offer great potential for dual mode imaging of malignant tumors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation and in vivo imaging of malignant tumors by the polymer-coated magnetic NPs.

Characterization of the magnetic nanoparticles. TEM images and hydrodynamic size distribution of (a, d) bare ferrites, (b, e) fluorescent magnets, and (c, f) polymer-coated hybrid NPs. The insets of TEM images show particle size distribution histograms determined by image analysis of NPs (n = 25).
HRTEM images of polymer-coated hybrid NPs.
Characterization of the polymer-coated hybrid NPs. (a) UV–Vis (b) PL, (c) Raman, and (d) FTIR spectra. (d) Magnetic hysteresis loops at room temperature.

Biocompatibility of polymer-coated hybrid NPs. (a) Cell viability at different concentrations of nanoparticles after 48 h and 72 h incubation. (b) H&E-stained sections on different tissues after 24 h injection with the nanoparticles (scale bare = 50 µm, magnification 100x).
In vivo fluorescence imaging of cancerous cells in mice. The tumor becomes visible with time.

MR imaging by magnetic nanoparticles. (a) T 1-weighted MR images of the magnetic particles at different Fe concentrations: (A) bare MnFe2O4, (B) N-CDs@MnFe2O4, and (C) N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween nanoparticles. A representative TR curve for the polymer-coated hybrid NPs. (b) Time-resolved T 2-weighted MR images of cancer tumor-bearing female BALB/c mice after injection of the polymer-coated hybrid NPs (the yellow circles show the area of the tumor).
Physiochemical properties and relaxivity measurements of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles synthesized in this work as compared with some reported MRI contrast agents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bimodal magnetic-fluorescent materials for diagnostic imaging needs surface-engineered nanoparticles with great biosafety, pronounced colloidal stability, high magnetic moments, and strong photoluminescence (PL) emission. This work presents polymer-coated nanoparticles (PCNPs) based on manganese ferrites covered with a thin shell of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for magnetic-resonance and fluorescent dual mode imaging of cancerous tumors in vivo. An in situ thermolysis of metal oxalates and phenylenediamine in diphenyl ether allows for the facile synthesis of hybrid magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles. They possess an average size of 55 ± 5 nm with strong and excitation-independent PL emission at about 600 nm with a saturation magnetization of ∼ 19 emu/g. In vivo dispersion and biosafety of the hybrid nanoparticles are improved by a polymer-coating strategy with the aid of Tween polysorbate. In vitro studies for SK-OV-3 cells reveal that PCNPs are not toxic up to a high concentration of 125 µg/mL. H&E-stained sections of vital organs of animals treated with dosages of 5 mg/kg fail to present any histopathological changes after 24 h post-injection. The fluorescence intensity in the tumor tissue region appears to increases over time and the tumor becomes clearly visible after 60 min. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of phantoms and animal cases also indicates the high efficiency of PCNPs as an T 2 contrast agent in clinical diagnosis. Therefore, the developed PCNPs offer great potential for dual mode imaging of malignant tumors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In clinical diagnostics, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides three-dimensional (3D) tomographic information with high temporal and spatial resolution [1–3]. Nevertheless, the low sensitivity of this technique caused by the long relaxation time of magnetic spins in water protons leads to unclear discrimination of normal and cancer tissues [4]. Magnetic nanoparticles (NPs) with uniform particle size, high superparamagnetic moment, and high colloidal stability as contrast agents increase image contrast by shortening longitudinal (T 1) and transverse (T 2) relaxation times [5–7]. In contrast to healthy tissue, cancer tissue cannot capture the contrast agents due to the lack of a reticuloendothelial system (RES) [8] hence, diagnosis of abnormalities requires both functional and cellular imaging techniques. For functional imaging, discrimination between cancer and normal tissues can be accomplished with T 2 contrast agents. Such T 2 contrast agent weakens the MR signals at that normal site while the signals remain unchanged at the cancer site [9]. Although dextran-coated superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (SPIONs) as T 2 and Gd3+ complexes as T 1 contrast agents are commonly employed for clinical applications [10–13], metal ferrites NPs (MFe2O4, M = Mn, Ni and Co) show higher r 2 (1/T 2) values than those of SPIONs and clinical agents [14]. As the saturation magnetization (M s) and r 2 values of ferrites increase within the sequence Ni < Co < Fe < Mn [15,16], contrast agents based on manganese ferrite have attracted much attention for MRI [17]. This is not only due to their higher magnetic susceptibility and saturation magnetization [18,19], but also their crystalline anisotropy which increases relaxation time compared with SPIONs [20]. In addition, when MnxFe3-xO4 NPs were used as a T 1 model contrast agent, it was found that T 1 relaxivities initially increases with increasing Mn doping [21].

Although the serial and non-invasive character of MRI allows the repeated imaging to further interrogate tissues, the lack of resolution at a cellular level is considered as a limitation for this technique [22]. Unlike MR imaging, fluorescence microscopy can provide greater resolution when investigating the behavior of fluorescent contrast agents at the cellular level [23]. As a result, the combination of magnetic and fluorescent properties in one compound introduces new bimodal “two-in-one” magnetic-fluorescent materials to provide accurate delineation of normal and abnormal tissues [24,25]. Generally, combined magnetic and fluorescent properties can be achieved by assembling magnetic NPs with fluorescent dyes (e.g., rhodamine and fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate (FITC)) [26,27], semiconductor quantum dots [28] and carbon-based materials [29]. Recently, fluorescent agents based on carbon dots (CDs) have attracted enormous interest because of their biocompatibility [30] and good resistance to photo-degradation and bleaching [31,32]. Functionalized-CDs with alcohols and thiols [33], polyethyleneimine [34] or diamines [35] not only exhibit enhanced fluorescence, but also have higher solubility in the biological medium [36], Experimental and theoretical studies have indicated that amine functionalized-CDs, so called nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), have identical abilities to label cells at a lower concentration than pristine CDs owing to their higher quantum yields [33]. Therefore, N-CDs are promising fluorescent alternatives, which render suitable candidates for diagnostic imaging [37].

The stabilization of NPs in aqueous solutions is one of the main concerns upon their biomedical applications, since NPs with hydrophobic surfaces tend to gradually aggregate in the body and to be immediately removed by the immune system [38]. Polymer coating is known as a promising strategy to improve the stability and in vivo circulation of NPs [39]. In fact, encapsulation of nanoparticles with polymeric chains can weaken interactions between the nanoparticles and plasma proteins, preventing protein corona formation that increases the mobility of injected nanoparticles in blood circulation [38]. Hydrophilic and biocompatible polymers with high affinity to NPs are desirable for the stabilization in the physiological environment [40]. Many studies have demonstrated beneficial effects of polymer coating by polyethylene glycol (PEG) [41], polysaccharide-based polymers [42], dextran [43], polyvinyl alcohol (PVP) [44], and poly(ethylene glycol)–co-fumarate (PEGF) [45] on in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility of magnetic NPs. Polysorbate 80 (Tween80) as a nontoxic, water soluble and electrically neutral polymer has a higher affinity to NPs through its oxy(hydroxy) and carbonyl functional groups than other common biocompatible polymers [46]. Studies have also shown that this polymer can easily bind to Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) receptors on the brain microvascular endothelial cells, facilitating delivery of biomolecules into the brain under the influence of a magnetic field [47,48].

In this work, a magneto-fluorescent contrast agent based on N-CDs, MnFe2O4 NPs, and Tween80 is introduced for in vivo dual-mode imaging of malignant tumors. To the best of our knowledge, synthesis of Tween80-coated N-CDs@MnFe2O4 NPs has not been yet reported and examined as a functional contrast agent. So far, thermal decomposition of metal–organic compounds by employing M(acac)x (M = Fe, Mn, Co, etc.) as a precursor in the presence of capping agents has been widely applied for the successful formation of monodisperse ferrites [49,50]. In this work, we describe a one-step thermal decomposition method for the synthesis of the hybrid magneto-fluorescent NPs through in situ thermolysis of metal-oxalate precursors and p-phenylenediamine in diphenyl ether at 200 °C. After precipitating magnetic cores capped with carbo shell, polymer encapsulation is carried out by dispersing N-CDs@MnFe2O4 NPs in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) containing Tween80 at room temperature. The application of PCNPs for diagnostic imaging of 4 T1 cancer in mice is documented by dual-mode MR and fluorescence imaging.

Manganese(II) oxalate dihydrate (Mn 30 % min) and iron(II) oxalate dihydrate (99.999%) were supplied from Alfa Aesar, Germany. Diphenyl ether (98.0%), 1,4-phenylenediamine (98.0%), ethanol (99.9%), n-hexane (96.0%), and Tween80 were purchased from Merck, Germany. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was prepared by dissolving adequate amounts of NaCl (99.5%, Merck), KCl (99.5%, Merck), Na2HPO4 (99.0%, Merck), and KH2PO4 (99.5%, Merck) in DI water (18 M Ohm) and adjusting the solution to the desired pH (∼7.4). For a cell viability assay, MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 98%) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Manganese(II) oxalate dihydrate and iron(II) oxalate dihydrate at a molar ratio of 1:2 were dispersed in diphenyl ether (30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 3 h under nitrogen atmosphere. When the color of the solution was changed to brown, 0.1 g 1,4-phenylenediamine was added into the solution, followed by stirring for 2 h. The precipitate was collected by a magnet after adding n-hexane into the solution. Finally, the product was washed with ethanol and n-hexane, and dried by a rotary evaporator (Hei-VAP Advantage, Heidolph, Germany).

The synthesized hybrid magnetic NPs (0.1 g) were dispersed in Tween80 solution (1% v/v in PBS) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The suspension was then dialyzed against DI water using a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 12,000 Da for 24 h. Finally, the functionalized nanoparticles were collected by a magnet, washed with DI water several times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 12 h.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded with a Thermo Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Nicolet Country Club, USA) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The size and morphology of the particles were characterized by Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin transmission electron microscopy (TEM, ICN2, Spain) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The mean size and zeta-potential of the particles were determined by a ZEN3600-nanoZS analyzer (Malvern, UK). Magnetic measurements were carried out at room temperature on a Physical Property Measurement System (PPMS, Quantum Design Inc., Germany). Raman spectra were recorded on a Jobin Yvon Horiba LAB-RAM spectrometer (Horiba, Japan) with 325 nm laser beam. UV–Vis absorption spectra were conducted on a Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded on a LS-55 Fluorophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined by a Philips diffractometer (Philips, Netherlands) using X’PertPro monochromatized CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å, operated on 35 mA and 40 kV current).

Cell toxicity was examined by a MTT assay [51] SK-OV-3 cells in their exponential growth phase were supplied by the Pasteur Institute of Iran. Approximately 3 × 103 cells were seeded in a flat-bottomed 96-well polystyrene-coated plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator (5%). The polymer-coated hybrid nanoparticles at different concentrations of 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL were added to the cell medium. After incubation for 24 or 48 h, 10 μL of the MTT reagent was added to each well and the plate was incubated for 4 h. The formed formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL DMSO and the plate was immediately read in a microplate reader (Bio-Rad model 680) at 560 nm. The control group was the incubated normal cells without nanoparticles. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

To assess the side effects of PCNPs on the liver, heart, kidney, lung and spleen, histopathology studies were performed by injecting different concentrations of NPs into 7–8 weeks old female BALB/c mice (weighting 23–25 g) supplied by the Pasteur Institute of Iran. After 24 h post-injection via tail vein, the animals were euthanized and the harvested tissues were fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin (NBF, pH = 7.26) for 48 h followed by processing and embedding in paraffin. Afterwards, cross-sections with a thickness of 5 µm were prepared and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The histological slides were evaluated by using light microscopy (Olympus BX51; Olympus, Tokyo, Japan). Any changes including acute and chronic inflammatory response, fatty change, coagulative necrosis, hemorrhage or hyperemia were assessed in different samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer tumor-bearing female BALB/c mice were supplied from the Pasteur Institute of Iran. Tumor growth was visible 3 weeks after post-injection of about 2 × 103 4 T1 cells. It is worth noted that at higher cell concentrations, the tumor appears at an earlier time. To detect the tumors under fluorescence microscopy (Fluovision, 16MP CCD camera, designed in TUMS Preclinical Core Faculty) with an excitation filter (390–630 nm) and an emission filter (450–800 nm), the shaved mice were anesthetized using 30–40 µL of ketamine (100 mg/kg)/xylazine (5 mg/kg). Subsequently, the dispersed PCNPs (5 mg/kg) in PBS (pH = 7.4) were injected via tail vein into the mice. The same approach was used for MR imaging. Herein, a clinical MRI system at 3.0 T (Magnetom Prisma; Siemens, Erlangen, Germany) with a mouse whole body coil was employed. Both in vivo florescence and MR imaging were performed at appropriate time points after injection.

MR imaging of NPs including bare MnFe2O4, N-CDs@MnFe2O4, and N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80 was carried out at 20 °C using 3.0 Tesla MRI scanner (Magnetom Prisma; Siemens, Erlangen, Germany). 1.0 mL of each NPs with different concentrations (1000, 500, 250, 125, and 62.5 µg/mL) were prepared and placed in the plastic phantom to determine the relaxation rates (1/T1 , 1/T2 ) as a function of iron concentration (mM). The values of the relaxivity (r1 , r2 ) were obtained from the assuming a mono-exponential signal decay. A conventional spin-echo pulse sequence was used for both T1 and T2 measurements with three slices of 4 mm thickness, a flip angle of 90°, the number of signal averages in 2, 128 × 128 mm field of view, and 128 × 128 matrices. T1 relaxation measurements were acquired with echo time (TE ) of 12 ms and repetition time (TR ) of 100, 300, 600, 1000, and 2000 ms. T2 relaxation measurements were performed using TE of 12, 30, 60, 100, 200, and 300 ms with a TR of 3000 ms. T2-weighted images were obtained with TR = 391 ms, TE = 2.6, 4.6, 6.6, 10.6, 12.6, 14.6, and 16.6 ms, FOV = 200 mm × 200 mm, slice thickness = 5 mm, and matrix = 192 × 192. The obtained raw-data were quantitatively analyzed and T1 and T2 curves and maps were computed. Quantitative measurements of T1 (longitudinal/spin–lattice relaxation) were performed using a least-square curve fitting on a pixel-by-pixel basis. For T1 and T2 curves, the signal intensity of each pixel was expressed by [52–55]:

One-way ANOVA (p < 0.05, n = 5) was carried out by employing the Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS software, Version 16, IBM, USA) to assess the statistically significant difference between measurements.
All animal studies were performed in compliance with the guidelines [58] approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Tehran University of Medical Sciences (TUMS).

Chemical synthesis and application of magnetic-fluorescence NPs for bioimaging are schematically presented in Fig. 1 . TEM studies have revealed that cubic MnFe2O4 particles with 10–12 nm sizes are formed (Fig. 2 a). In-situ synthesis of the magnetic particles in the presence of the carbon precursor resulted in the formation of an ellipsoid-like carbon shell around the MnFe2O4 cores (Fig. 2b). The thickness of the carbon shells ranged from 6 to 8 nm. Introducing the polysorbate polymer capped the core–shell NPs with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups and formed an inorganic/organic hybrid system with sizes between 55 and 60 nm (Fig. 2c). According to the particle size distribution determined by TEM images (the inserts of Fig. 2a-c), particle sizes of MnFe2O4, N-CDs@MnFe2O4, and N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80 are 10.02 ± 2.25, 17.88 ± 1.30, and 60.45 ± 2.55 nm, respectively. Determination of the hydrodynamic size by DLS (Fig. 2d-f) yields values of 10–12 ± 1.0 nm (MnFe2O4), 16–18 ± 1.0 nm (N-CDs@MnFe2O4), and 60–62 ± 1.0 nm (N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80) with PDIs ≤ 0. 5. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) presented in Fig. 3 a-c reveals more details about the morphology of the polymer-coated hybrid NPs. As seen in Fig. 3a, estimated particle sizes of the NPs are between 50 and 60 nm. Fig. 3b and 3c show that the MnFe2O4 and N-doped CQDs have crystalline lattice fringes. The 0.21 nm and 0.256 nm distances could be attributed to the (100) [59] and (311) [60] lattice of graphene and cubic MnFe2O4, respectively.

The surface charge of NPs was studied by Zeta measurements (Fig. S1). The bare MnFe2O4 particles have a high negative surface charge (-50 ± 2.5 mV) originating from the oxygen anions located on the corners of the cubic unite cells [61] as well as the presence of negatively charged groups (e.g., OH and COOH) on their surface formed during thermal decomposition of metal oxalates [29]. After capping with N-CDs, the surface charge is decreased to –22 ± 2.0 mV. Meanwhile, a weak peak is still detectable at around −50 mV (Fig. S1b). It appears that some of the magnetic cores are not completely covered by the carbon shell, as also noticed in TEM studies (Fig. 2b). Polymer coating reduces the surface charge of the hybrid NPs to −17 ± 1.0 mV (Fig. S1c), owing to interactions between the functional groups of N-CDs (amine) and Tween80 (hydroxyl and carbonyl) [46].

The absorption spectrum of PCNPs shows two clear bands at 206 and 327 nm, which are attributed to π-π* transitions of C = C bonds in the aromatic structure of N-CDs (Fig. 4 a). PL spectra of PCNPs at different excitation wavelengths (EW) determine a relatively broad and EW-independent emission at ≥ 600 nm (Fig. 4b). The PL emission originates from oxygen vacancies and intramolecular charge transfers [62,63]. The estimated quantum yield of N-CDs@MnFe2O4 and PCNPs is about 39% and 35%, respectively, under 530 nm excitation in absolute ethanol (Fig. S2a-c). The high QY of the products can be related to the nitrogen doped CDs, which are generally excellent auxochromes and show a drastic increase in the QY compared to the non-nitrogen CDs (QY = 0.5–1.8%) [57]. To investigate the fluorescence stability of the samples, the PL intensity of N-CDs@MnFe2O4 and PCNPs under 360 nm excitation was evaluated and compared for 200 min (Fig. S2d). Although the PL intensity of PCNPs is lower than that of N-CDs@MnFe2O4 at the same conditions, the changes of the PL intensity are nearly constant for PCNPs during 140 min, indicating high fluorescence stability of PCNPs for bio-applications.

Raman spectroscopy reveals characteristic peaks at 1388, 1492 and 2833 cm−1 (Fig. 4c). These peaks are ascribed to the disorder in the sp 2 hybridized carbon (D band), first-order scattering of the stretching vibration E2g mode of sp 2 carbon (G band), and phonon scattering at the boundaries and defects (2D band) of carbon materials, respectively [64]. The peaks observed below 800 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching modes of manganese ferrite (A1g, Eg, T2g) with spinel structure [65]. The FTIR spectrum of the nanoparticles (Fig. 4d) shows stretching vibrations of M−O (M = Mn and Fe) bonds at tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sites of the spinel structure around 400 to 660 cm−1 [66]. The characteristic absorption peaks of the polymer are detected at 1000–1150 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of C-O) and at 2860 cm−1 and 2926 cm−1 (symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH2 group) [67]. The absorption peaks at 1557 and 3740 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = N/C = O and N-H bands of the N-functionalized carbon dots [37]. To study the chemical composition of the NPs, XRD patterns of bare MnFe2O4 (Fig. S3a) and polymer-coated N-CDs@MnFe2O4 (Fig. S3b) indicate the formation of the cubic phase of MnFe2O4 with the space group of Fd-3 m and cell constants a = b = c = 8.5150 Å (JCPDS No. 73–1964). The noisy XRD pattern of Fig. S3b can be ascribed to the presence of polymer on the surface of the nanoparticles. A similar observation has also been reported for tetraethylene glycol-stabilized MnFe2O4 nanoparticle [68], dextran-coated iron oxide NPs [69], and PEG-coated metal oxide NPs [70].

Magnetic hysteresis loops of NPs exhibit superparamagnetic behavior with negligible magnetic remanence (M r) and coercive force (H c) (Fig. 4e and Table 1 ). The saturated magnetization (M s) of MnFe2O4 NPs is about 46 emu/g that is close to the M s values of the MnFe2O4 NPs with particle sizes of about 7 nm (39 emu/g) [68] and 16 nm (41 emu/g) [71]. The M s value of MnFe2O4 NPs is distinctly lower than the one reported for the bulk MnFe2O4 (80 emu/g) [72] due to the presence of an inactive magnetic layer or a disordered layer on the surfaces of the NPs [73,74]. On the other hand, the lower M s value of bare MnFe2O4 NPs compared to the NPs synthesized by thermal decomposition of M(acac)x [49,50] can be attributed to the structural defects and vacancies formed during the synthesis method. The presence of the carbon shell has significantly reduced the magnetic permeability with a slight change in M s (19 emu/g). The reduced saturation is attributed to a shielding effect of the carbon on the magnetic properties of NPs [29,75]. The saturation magnetization of PCNPs was determined to be 12 emu/g. The reduced magnetization originated from the non-magnetic contribution of the polymer shell with lower magneto-crystalline anisotropy and disordered surface spins [76]. The results indicated that surface modification of superparamagnetic MnFe2O4 NPs reduced Mr , Ms and Hc values. The N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80 nanoparticles exhibited paramagnetic behavior. Polymer modification changed the surface-to-volume ratio and induces “spin disorder” at the surface of NPs. Berkowitz et al. [77,78] have proposed the “spin canting” and “spin pinning” models for ferrite nanoparticles modified with organic compounds. The have shown that spin disorder can change the magnetic properties of nanoparticles, especially when the changes in surface/volume ratio are not negligible. Although polymer coating reduces Mr , Ms , and Hc values compared to NPs, the amount of saturation magnetization is still sufficient for biological applications as a contrast agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo biocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration-dependent MTT assay has shown that up to 62.5 µg/mL, the number of survived cells in contact with the PCNPs is ≥ 85% of the initial value after 48 h incubation (Fig. 5 a). Although the cell viability gradually decreases with increasing the nanoparticle concentration, IC50 (the concentration of NPs that is required for 50% inhibition of the cell viability) is beyond 1000 µg/mL, indicating the biosafety of PCNPs in vitro.

In order to study the in vivo biocompatibility of PCNPs, the nanoparticles were injected into the model animals at two concentrations of 5 and 10 mg/kg. Fig. 5b shows the results of histological analyses of H&E-stained tissues. The histopathological micrographs of all harvested samples from the control and injected mice with 5 mg/kg dosage are normal without any histopathological changes. However, at the concentration of 10 mg/kg, histopathological evaluations of the lung show a mild pulmonary hemorrhage and hyperemia. Degeneration of hepatocytes (hydropic degeneration) is also noticeable in liver. Generally, most of the nanoparticles injected into blood veins tend to exhibit high uptake in spleen, liver and lungs as the reticuloendothelial system (RES), and they are not rapidly excreted by the kidney [68]. Hence, we have considered 5 mg/kg as the safe concentration for in vivo bioimaging since the nanoparticles do not damage the lung tissue and cause acute hepatic cell injury.

The toxicity mechanism of magnetic NPs with different chemical compositions and morphologies has comprehensively been studied by Pati et al. [39] Markides et al. [82], and Mahmoudi et al. [83] The main cause of toxicity is ascribed to oxidative stress arising when there is no balance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals and hydrogen peroxide, and protective antioxidants such as vitamin C and glutathione [39]. Leaching of metal ions or release of oxidants by enzymatic degradation of NPs can generate ROS, which may lead to impair cell metabolism and increase in apoptosis [84]. On the other hand, after injection of magnetic NPs into the blood fluid, plasma proteins start binding onto their surfaces. The protein corona formation reduces in vivo circulation and stimulates the immune system through ROS production as a major reason behind cell death [39,85]. Studying the effect of temperature and surface charge on protein corona formation by iron oxides reveals that the greatest ROS level is attained by NPs with negative and neutral surface charges at 37 °C, while positive NPs show their maximum at 39 °C [86]. On the other hand, in vivo biodistribution studies on PEG-coated iron oxide NPs demonstrate that positively charged nanoparticles are more toxic and accumulate preferentially in lungs of mice, while the negatively charged NPs have a high tendency to localize in liver [87]. We have found that the high negative surface charge of MnFe2O4 NPs could remarkably be reduced by N-CDs- and Tween-coating (Fig. S1c), thereby improving cytocompatibility and biodistribution.

PCNPs were used for fluorescence imaging of a cancerous tumor in model animals. Fig. 6 shows the image of the whole body (before excitation) and tumor at different times of post-injection. Since the quality of the autofluorescence signals associated with the tissue is not significant, almost no signal is recorded in pre-injection state. When PCNPs were injected into the mice, the fluorescence intensity in the tumor tissue region increased with time. As a result, the tumor became clearly distinguishable at 60–75 min post-injection. Interestingly, no emitted signal is detectable in the inner region of the tumor, probably due to the highly abnormal or absence of blood vessels in tumors [88]. Obviously, the injected PCNPs in blood circulation possess a tendency to enter into the tumor via leaky blood vessels and to accumulate, thereby providing a suitable platform for in vivo imaging [89]. Although the PL peak around 600 nm cannot be used for deep imaging as well as IR-emitting NPs, the results of in vivo fluorescent imaging confirmed the potential of the NPs for bio-imaging. It is important to mention that N-doped CDs may exhibit near IR emission (up to 700 nm) depending on the synthesis condition and dispersing solvent [63]. The PL emission peak shows a red-shift as the solvent polarity increases due to the intramolecular charge transfer. In addition, the presence of the magnetic core and Tween80 can also effect on PL emission of CDs through the changes in the HOMO/LOMO levels, making quenching phenomena [90].

In order to determine the potential application of PCNPs for MR imaging, relaxivity measurements for bare MnFe2O4, N-CDs@MnFe2O4, and N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80 NPs at different Fe concentrations (below 1000 µg/mL) were performed (Fig. 7 a). Analysis of relaxation rates via plotting 1/T 2 and 1/T 1 versus Fe concentration (Fig. S4) determines that the relaxation rate significantly increases from 10 to 37 s-1mM−1 for r 1 and from 81 to 458 s-1mM−1 for r 2 in the order of MnFe2O4 < N-CDs@MnFe2O4 < N-CDs@MnFe2O4/Tween80 (Table 1). The ratio of r 2/r 1 also increases from 8.10 to 12.4. Previous studies have shown that lower values of r 2/r 1 (between 1–3 s-1mM−1) support T 1-positive, higher values (>10 s-1mM−1) lead to T 2-negative, and the intermediate values (3–10 s-1mM−1) can be considered as dual T 1 and T 2 contrast agents [91]. Thus, our results suggest that PCNPs are suitable for T 2 contrast imaging with reduced relaxation times and high contrast [92]. The high r 2/r 1 ratio also determines that a better contrast can be attained by PCNPs as compared to recently developed magnetic agents (Table 1). Sharmiladevi et al. [79] have demonstrated that the intensity of T 2-weighted images in phantom MR imaging by SiO2@MnFe2O4 is higher than that of uncoated MnFe2O4 NPs due to high interactions between water molecules and the particle core. In PCNPs, interactions of the hydrophilic chains of Tween80 provide higher r 2 and r 2/r 1 values than in case of the bare and N-CDs coated magnetic NPs [79–81].

To evaluate the ability of PCNPs for in vivo imaging, the nanoparticles were employed for MRI of malignant tumors in female mice. Fig. 7b displays typical T 2-weighted MR images of a tumor before and after injection. MR images of post-injected mice show obvious darkening in the tumor site over time. The results indicate that the nanoparticles can penetrate the blood vessel of the tumor and specifically accumulate in the tissue by the enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect [93,94].

In summary, a facile thermolysis approach was developed to prepare core–shell NPs for in vivo dual mode imaging. Nano-sized MnFe2O4 magnetic cores (8–10 nm) were covered by a thin sell of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (6–8 nm) and a polysorbate polymer shell to afford hybrid magneto-fluorescent NPs with an average diameter of 55–60 nm and negative surface charge of about 17 mV. In vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity studies revealed a distinct concentration-dependent effect of PCNPs on cell viability and tissue damage. The nanoparticles exhibited acceptable cell viability (>80%) at concentrations below 125 µg/mL. According to in vivo MR experiments, PCNPs enhanced T 2 relaxation and provided a distinct contrast after 30 min post-injection. In vivo tumor imaging by fluorescence microscopy also indicated that a malignant tumor clearly became distinguishable at 60 min post-injection. The results of this study showed the clinical potential of applying PCNPs as a dual-imaging model for discriminating and locating tumors.
